content_html:
Last time, we looked at how the owner of a dialog can take control just before the dialog box message loop goes idle. I said that I pulled a trick.

The trick is that I used the common file open dialog instead of a simple MessageôÙBox. Indeed, if you replace the call to GetôÙOpenôÙFileôÙName with a call to MessageôÙBox, then no WM_ENTERôÙIDLE message arrives, and you get no beeping. What’s going on?

A dialog can suppress the WM_ENTERôÙIDLE message by adding the DS_NOôÙIDLEôÙMSG dialog style to its template. And that’s what the template used by the MessageôÙBox function does.

So the WM_ENTERôÙIDLE trick does require a small amount of cooperation from the dialog box, namely that it doesn’t disable WM_ENTERôÙIDLE messages.

But say you can guarantee the cooperation of the dialog box because you are the dialog box. Right now, the WM_ENTERôÙIDLE message allows a dialog owner to be notified when the dialog message loop is about to go idle. But what if the dialog box itself wants to know, so it can customize its own message loop?

We’ll look at that next time.

The post Why doesn’t <CODE>WM_<WBR>ENTER­IDLE</CODE> work if the dialog box is a <CODE>Message­Box</CODE>? appeared first on The Old New Thing.

summary: Because it opted out. The post Why doesnãt WM_ENTERôÙIDLE work if the dialog box is a MessageôÙBox? appeared first on The Old New Thing .
